data.title: FIAX: A Hedge-Fund Like Structure Which Under-Delivers
data.description:
Thesis

The Nicholas Fixed Income Alternative ETF (FIAX) is a fund we started covering a year ago, when we went into detail in respect to the fund structure and composition. At the time we stated the following:

A retail investor, however, needs to understand the ETF is taking equity risk factors in order to generate fixed income returns. The equity risk has capped downside and upside, but could also represent a drag on performance if the market moves against the fund. FIAX represents an alternative in the space as long as the managers deliver on the market risk taken.

In today's article we are going to revisit the fund in light of its 2025 performance and risk factors and outline our view for the fund in the new macro paradigm.

What does the fund do, and what does it hold

Let us first revisit the fund objective so investors have a refreshed view on that:

The Nicholas Fixed Income Alternative ETF Fund is an actively managed exchange-traded fund (“ETF”) that seeks to provide income using U.S. Treasury fixed income securities and a “defined risk option premium.”

The Fund’s defined risk option premium strategy uses options on ETFs across multiple asset classes (e.g., equities, commodities, fixed income). Through the defined risk option premium strategy, two options transactions are paired together in order to create a “defined risk” trade that caps the maximum possible gains and losses from the outset.

So FIAX works like a mini hedge fund that parks most of its cash in treasuries, while taking capped market risk via option structures. We have seen this structure before in the Simplify Enhanced Income ETF (HIGH), which we cover on the Seeking Alpha platform.

These sorts of funds ultimately rely on the portfolio managers' acumen in order to trade good risk. We called FIAX a mini hedge fund because its risk factors can change every day as the managers see fit. In our last article, we outlined a long semiconductor position the fund was taking via call spreads. Right now, for example, the ETF is long utilities via a call spread option structure:

The XLU ticker is the State Street® Utilities Select Sector SPDR® ETF (XLU), and the fund is long the 38 strike call and short the 45 strike call in the amount of 1600 contracts. The expiry date for both legs is March 20, 2026. If XLU does not rally into the respective range at the expiry, the fund loses the paid premium. If the fund does rally into the range, then the structure can make money.

The takeaway for an investor here is that track record matters for FIAX because its risk factors are not unilateral, changing basically every day as managers see fit. The only reliable factors here are represented by performance and analytics, factors that we are going to analyze next.

Disappointing performance in the past year

If we look at what FIAX did in the past year, we can observe a pattern:

Just like its brethren, Simplify Enhanced Income ETF, FIAX got burned in the risk-off market from April 2025, and never got back on its feet. In fact, the total return for FIAX is converging with HIGH, which is disappointing for both names.

The ETF takes proprietary market positions (albeit with capped risk), and thus relies on the market factors moving in the desired direction in order to make money. It seems the managers never put on significant correct risk after the April/May 2025 event, thus never even managed to outperform simple cash in 2025.

The only way to measure a fund like FIAX is through actual performance and analytics. The name cycled through many long and short positions via options, but did not manage to create positive results. In fact, the options positions created a negative total return, since the fund overall produced a result inferior to just holding treasuries outright.

Analytics

Despite its poor performance, the fund managed to maintain a tight volatility profile:

The ETF has a low standard deviation of 3.9%, and a low annualized volatility of 4.17%. This is due to the usage of call and put spreads, structures that are intrinsically capped in terms of gains and losses.

While a low volatility profile is good, one does need to produce high total returns in order to obtain a high Sharpe ratio. It is not the case here, with FIAX clocking in a Sharpe of -0.37. A negative Sharpe ratio means that an investment’s return was lower than the risk-free rate; thus, an investor would have been better off keeping their money in a government bond.

So with FIAX, we are looking at a low risk and very low return type of investment. The main risk factor here is the ability of the portfolio managers to put winning positions on the books, thus the hedge fund-like nature of this ETF. Unfortunately, the name has done very poorly, and the cost of opportunity is very high in today's market. For us there is no reason to underwrite this name and the managers when there are many other opportunities out there to generate significant yields. The most we could tolerate would be a total return in line with outright cash, but it is not the case here. The fund actually subtracted from a pure cash position via the options trades.

Conclusion

FIAX is an exchange-traded fund from the XFunds family. The name runs a core treasuries position and layers options spreads on top similarly to a hedge fund. FIAX has not been able to generate a positive return in the past year from its options positions, although it has kept its volatility profile fairly low. With a negative Sharpe ratio and underwhelming results, we see no reason to keep capital allocated here.

data.title: SkyWater Technology: One Year Later, The Easy Money Is Gone
data.description:
When I wrote about SkyWater Technology, Inc. (SKYT) last January, the appeal was pretty simple. The business was strategically important, very early in its scaling journey, and priced like that was its final form, so to speak. I think the market was looking at its choppy Advanced Technology Services (ATS) revenue and uneven profitability while not fully recognizing the long-term value of a trusted, domestic foundry model running with customer-funded capex.

It’s a year later now, and SKYT has more than doubled from the top end of the valuation range I was comfortable with at the time. That move has come from the market treating it like the strategic manufacturing asset that it really is, and I believe that it is now priced just right, so my posture has shifted to a Hold. In this piece, I want to look at the company’s performance with integration, execution, and delivery at a larger scale.

What Has Changed Over The Last Year?

SkyWater is still the same kind of business as it was 12 months ago, but it is much bigger and more consistent. It is a US-based specialty foundry that sits away from leading-edge nodes and focuses instead on trusted manufacturing, co-development, and long-cycle programs. The mix of wafer services and advanced technology services is still central to its business, and most of the demand still comes from customers who care more about security, reliability, and domestic supply than bleeding-edge performance.

I’d divide 2025 into two halves when discussing the company because of its Fab 25 acquisition. I’ll also refer to its business segments as Legacy SkyWater and SkyWater Texas, as management did in the Q3 2025 earnings report. The former didn’t perform particularly well in the first half of the year: revenue from its Tools section fell pretty sharply compared to 2024’s numbers, and ATS spending from aerospace and defense was very uneven because of contract timing issues.

I will say that I think the acquisition is the single most important thing it did last year because before that, it was essentially a one-fab company in Minnesota built around a mix of ATS and specialty wafer manufacturing. After buying the 200mm wafer Fab 25 facility in Austin, Texas, for a total of $93 million from Infineon Technologies in June 2025, the numbers changed pretty dramatically. The acquisition came with an anchor customer and a multi-year wafer supply agreement that includes minimum volume commitments. When I was reviewing the earnings materials from the last few quarters, the one thing that really jumped out was how much the fab changed SKYT’s trajectory, and we’ve seen it in the results.

In Q3 FY25, the Texas facility generated $86.6 million of wafer services revenue, way more than the entire legacy business produced in any of the preceding three quarters. So, the company is now running with manufacturing-led revenue, and that gives it more visibility into utilization rates so it can plan better. Additionally, SKYT doesn’t have to rely solely on ATS timing to deliver strong quarterly results, so the operating model is now more complex and scalable. That distinction is crucial for how I see the company’s execution risk and valuation moving forward.

Of course, ATS is still a key part of the story, and it is doing its part by pulling the company into programs early. Customer-funded tooling still limits how much capital SKYT can risk upfront, too, but everything is now supporting a much bigger footprint. I like that.

The Numbers Don’t Lie

When I step back and look at the financials since my original piece, the most important thing to understand is that SkyWater’s growth over the last year is real, but it is also uneven by design. The headline change is scale. In Q3 FY25, the company’s total consolidated revenue reached $150.7 million, up 61% year over year, and that jump is almost entirely because SkyWater Texas came online and contributed $86.6 million of wafer services revenue in its first full quarter as part of the company.

On the other hand, Legacy SkyWater only generated $64.1 million, only slightly better than the previous two quarters’ average of $60 million. Still, it was down over 30% year over year, which explains why the surface-level growth story looks confusing if you don’t split the segments out.

Now, I want to discuss how the legacy business’s own segments performed. ATS development revenue came in at $54.2 million, slightly higher than in Q2 but still lower on a YoY basis. That decline is not a demand issue, and management pulled roughly $4 million of ATS revenue forward into Q3 because of aerospace and defense program timing. Meanwhile, the company was also contending with some delays in broader government funding approvals. At the same time, the revenue from wafer services in Minnesota actually grew sequentially, but it’s still a small piece of the legacy mix.

If we look at Tools revenue, its performance wasn’t surprising either. It collapsed year over year, falling 88% from $30.7 million in Q3 FY24 to $3.7 million in Q3 FY25. This was mostly because the segment’s sales depend entirely on customer procurement decisions and don’t repeat cleanly. It was pretty much the same story in Q1 and Q2.

With margins, the story is a bit more interesting. Non-GAAP gross margin rose to 24.6% in Q3, up more than 500 basis points sequentially, and that boost came from a combination of higher Texas wafer volumes, favorable purchase accounting effects, and about $5 million in non-recurring cost reversals. Obviously, some of those benefits are a one-time thing, so we cannot fully attribute the margin improvement to the business’s performance. That’s fine, but it also showed how much operating leverage exists when utilization improves. Besides that, adjusted EBITDA reached $25.8 million (17.1% of revenue), way, way above expectations.

The Value Setup Is Different Now

SkyWater’s current valuation is far removed from where it was when I called a Buy, and justifiably so. Back then, the stock was cheap because the market didn’t believe scale would show up, at least not that quickly. Today, that scale is here, and the stock is priced accordingly.

Let us consider things on a revenue basis, normalizing Q3 FY25 as a starting point and thinking about FY26 as the first full year with Texas. That would bring FY26 revenues well over $600 million, and using the current share base of roughly 48.5 million shares outstanding, we get revenue per share around $12.5. That’s a big jump from last January, when that ratio was closer to $7 with roughly the same share count, so clearly, the underlying equity value is a lot stronger.

Today, the stock is also trading at ~3.7x forward P/S, well above the re-rating I argued for last year, too. With EBITDA, the story is a bit different. Yes, SKYT’s adjusted EBITDA in Q3 FY25 was strong on paper, but you must keep in mind that it benefited from a few one-off benefits too. Right now, its EV/EBITDA multiple is more than double the sector median of ~16x, which is a pretty hefty premium for a company that is just starting to scale revenue, and we need to see that performance repeated consistently. You might not think it’s expensive, in isolation, but at these levels and compared to the general industry multiples, it is difficult to argue that the stock hasn’t outrun the business at this stage.

Finally, we can’t ignore dilution. I mentioned the share count earlier, and it has crept up very modestly over the past year, mostly because of equity compensation. SKYT hasn’t used its ATM much during FY25, which is good, but again, the reality is that the per-share math now assumes execution at a larger scale. The market isn’t paying these prices for a company that may still struggle with operational hiccups or unstable margins–execution is the driver here–and I can’t see any margin of safety at the current price.

What I’m Watching In Q4: Catalysts

Q4 is the first quarter where SkyWater is guiding the business with Fab 25 fully in the picture, and the targets are pretty clear. You can find them below.

Here’s what I’m looking out for when those Q4 results come out in a few weeks.

I’m watching whether Texas holds the line at high-$80M-ish revenue without needing any surprise WIP dynamics to make the quarter look good. In the Q3 deck, management explained that Texas beat expectations partly because of a higher-than-forecast off-market component and additional revenue from work completed on the elevated WIP immediately post-acquisition. That’s fine, but it also means that Q4 is where the run-rate starts to matter more. We can't have one-off benefits clouding the true picture of things.

I’m also keeping an eye on Tools. The segment has been unstable for a hot minute, and management is guiding $17M–$18M in Q4 tools revenue. During the Q3 FY25 earnings call, they noted that one tool shifts to 2026, which tells me the lumpiness hasn’t gone anywhere. Some stability would be great, and it is crucial for me to be able to assign a value to Tools as a major contributor moving forward.

And then there’s margins. Q3 non-GAAP gross margin printed at 24.6%, way above what they guided, but Q4 guidance (17%–20% non-GAAP GM) is basically them telling us not to anchor to Q3 as the new normal. It’s even more poignant when you remember that Q4 still benefits from some carryover, so we might be dealing with a lower figure when everything normalizes. I want to see that number averaging around the 20, 21% mark before I can put any weighting to it as a catalyst.

I’m Bullish On The Business But Not Enough To Bite At This Price

I’ve laid out why I’m not convinced that $33/share is a good price to buy into SKYT, even though the fundamentals are much better now than they were a year ago. I've also listed the things I need to see over a sustained period to get off the fence--I’m talking financially and operationally. For me, Q4 is a proof quarter, and even if the company achieves what it laid out in guidance, I’d still be waiting for consistent performance over a few quarters before reassigning a Buy to the stock. I believe that at this level, patience is more important than chasing a hot stock.

data.title: Parnassus Value Select ETF Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
The strategy seeks to achieve strong long-term returns through a high-conviction, highly concentrated portfolio of approximately 25 undervalued U.S. large-cap stocks that are poised to rise but are temporarily out of favor relative to their history or peers.

Market Review

The Russell 1000 Value Index ended a strong year with solid gains in the fourth quarter and double-digit returns for the year as investors continued to focus on the ongoing megatrend in AI. This investment theme continues to surprise to the upside, demonstrating that it is likely here to stay. The Federal Reserve (Fed) cut interest rates for the third time in 2025, against the backdrop of a relatively steady economy with modest GDP growth, a relatively strong labor market and moderating inflation, although the benefits weren’t felt equally across the economy. These stable economic conditions and strong earnings growth also helped to boost markets. During the quarter, the best-performing sectors in the Russell 1000 Value Index were Information Technology, Communication Services and Health Care, while the Real Estate and Utilities sectors ended the quarter with slight losses. The strong returns across a range of sectors reflected the broadening of the AI theme, not just the continuation of it.

Quarterly Performance Review

Broadening sector strength boosted relative performance

The Fund returned 5.85% (NAV) for the quarter, outperforming the Russell 1000 Value Index’s 3.81%. The outperformance was notably driven by our Utilities and Industrials holdings and an Information Technology overweight, fueled by positive sentiment toward the latest AI developments. The relative gains were partially offset by holdings in the Materials and Consumer Discretionary sectors.

Top Quarterly Contributors

Micron Technology (MU) shares outperformed due to the company's continued strong position in the AI-driven memory market. Management noted a robust demand environment for its HBM DRAM chips.

Applied Materials (AMAT) shares climbed amid the accelerating adoption of AI and the subsequent demand for advanced semiconductors and wafer fab equipment.

AstraZeneca (AZN) advanced due to a combination of positive clinical trial results for key drugs and significant expansion of its U.S. manufacturing footprint.

Cummins (CMI) rallied after the engines and industrial equipment maker reported strong third-quarter sales results due to strong growth in the distribution and power systems segment driven by data center demand.

Alphabet (GOOGL) posted strong performance in the quarter as resilient revenues for its search business, improving growth in its cloud segment and renewed confidence in its vertically integrated AI strategy lifted investor sentiment.

Bottom Quarterly Contributors

NICE (NICE), a software company providing contact center solutions, lost investor confidence due to concerns over AI disruption. However, we believe that the company should see AI transform its business model and drive long-term growth.

Home Depot (HD) shares declined due to continued pressure from housing market weakness and lower confidence in consumer spending. However, the company remains well positioned to capture upside from a potential recovery in the home improvement market.

Microsoft (MSFT) shares detracted as Wall Street worried over the company’s heavy investments in AI along with increasing competition from Google’s Gemini 3.0. However, the company remains in pole position to benefit from enterprise AI adoption.

Sysco (SYY) shares dropped after management's conservative outlook for fiscal year 2026 due to continued macro uncertainties and uneven demand in the "food-away-from-home" sector.

Verizon Communications (VZ) shares detracted after the surprise replacement of CEO Hans Vestberg with former PayPal CEO Dan Schulman in early October. Investors also turned away from defensive stocks during the quarter.

Annual Performance Review

Tech holdings fueled relative performance

For the one-year period, the Fund returned 18.06% (NAV), beating the Russell 1000 Value’s 15.91%. The portfolio’s holdings in Information Technology primarily contributed to outperformance, while positions in the Utilities and Real Estate sectors also helped. Conversely, holdings in Health Care and Consumer Discretionary offset some of those gains. Notably, there was improved sentiment in Health Care at the end of the year despite overall weakness in 2025.

Top Annual Contributors

Micron Technology shares outperformed due to the company's strong position in the AI-driven memory market. Management noted robust demand environment for its HBM DRAM chips.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSMC) (TSM) shares rose as demand for advanced AI chips continued to grow. Its dominant industry position made it a primary beneficiary of sustained AI infrastructure investment.

Applied Materials benefited from sustained AI-driven semiconductor demand, with improving customer outlooks supporting the stock.

AstraZeneca advanced due to a combination of positive clinical trial results for key drugs and significant expansion of its U.S. manufacturing footprint.

Cummins, the engines and industrial equipment maker, saw its shares rally due to strong growth in the distribution and power systems segment driven by data center demand and its shift in focus to renewable energy solutions.

Bottom Annual Contributors

NICE, a software company providing contact center solutions, lost investor confidence due to concerns over AI disruption. However, we believe that the company should see AI transform its business model and drive long-term growth.

UnitedHealth Group (UNH) shares declined over the year amid rising costs, increased regulatory scrutiny and the abrupt resignation of Chief Executive Andrew Witty. We are optimistic that execution will significantly improve with the return of former CEO Stephen Hemsley.

Bio-Rad Laboratories (BIO) underperformed in 2025 amid high uncertainty for academic funding for life sciences research and development.

Comcast (CMCSA) shares underperformed as weakness in its broadband business offset growth in other divisions. We exited our position in Comcast for other opportunities.

Agilent Technologies (A), a supplier of equipment for life science research, was under pressure as recent cuts to grants from the National Institutes of Health impacted its near-term outlook. We exited our position earlier in the year.

Portfolio Positioning

Portfolio anchored on quality amid elevated market valuations

We continue to maintain a balanced, quality-anchored positioning in the portfolio. Our bottom-up fundamental assessment drives this risk-aware exposure across industries that we believe will benefit from long-term thematic tailwinds. We also maintain ample exposure to defensive holdings that will preserve capital should markets experience increased volatility or a pullback in the coming quarters.

The Fund's largest sector overweights are in Information Technology, Health Care and Communications Services. Our three largest underweights are in Energy (no exposure), Consumer Staples and Consumer Discretionary.

Our overweight in Information Technology was modestly lower versus the third quarter as we continue to realize gains in select long-term secular winners. While we remain constructive on the sector’s structural growth drivers, we are also cognizant of valuation levels, particularly as market gains have broadened across a wider array of beneficiaries in recent quarters.

During the quarter, we modestly increased our overweight in the Health Care sector with the addition of Abbott Laboratories (ABT). We like Abbott’s leadership position in diabetes care, heart health and electrophysiology, all supported by ongoing innovation and new product launches. Overall, we like the attractive risk/reward opportunity in Health Care, especially as the sector emerges from several headwinds faced over the past three years.

We reduced our overweight in the Communications Services sector with the exit of Comcast. We divested from Comcast due to a deterioration in our investment thesis as cable companies face rising competition from wireless providers.

Outlook

Market environment favors continued outperformance for value stocks

Value stocks outperformed growth stocks in the fourth quarter as the Russell 1000 Value index rose 3.81% compared to a 1.12% rise in the Russell 1000 Growth index. We expect the current market environment to continue favoring value stocks. High market valuations for growth stocks, a relatively benign economic backdrop, potential for lower rates and stimulative policy levers are all supportive of attractive risk/reward potential in value stocks.

Moreover, we believe the once-in-a-generation AI investments being made today will, over time, drive productivity gains, business model innovation and earnings growth across a much broader range of industries than is being appreciated by investors today. AI has the potential to impact every sector, not just technology, similar to earlier waves of automation and technology adoption. The timing of these benefits admittedly remains uncertain, which underpins the Fund's diversified exposure across sectors and across time—some will benefit this year, some over three years or longer. However, what we believe is more certain is that regardless of the sector, companies that have a strong track record of investing in innovation and the scale to justify these upfront investments will eventually emerge stronger through AI. This was true in prior innovation cycles, and this time will be no different.

The high-quality companies that we own in the Value Select ETF—across Banking, Payments, Pharma, Medical Devices or Agricultural Equipment, among others—all possess the scale to invest ahead of peers and a demonstrated ability to translate investments into enduring shareholder value.

Our time-tested investment process with a disciplined focus on both risk and return has served us well over the last two decades. We remain confident that the portfolio is well positioned to navigate both macroeconomic and thematic crosscurrents in the coming years. We will continue to focus on identifying quality companies trading at attractive relative valuations in the pursuit of strong long-term returns.

Glossary

Earnings Growth is the change in an company’s reported net income over a period of time.

Allocation Effect: The allocation effect in performance attribution measures how much of a portfolio's outperformance or underperformance against a benchmark comes from the manager's decision to overweight or underweight specific sectors or asset classes, rather than from picking individual stocks within them.

Russell 1000® Growth Index: The Russell 1000® Growth Index measures the performance of the large cap growth segment of the U.S. equity universe. An individual cannot invest directly in an index. An index reflects no deductions for fees, expenses or taxes, but mutual fund returns do.

Important Information

The Russell 1000® Value Index is a market capitalization weighted index that measures the performance of those Russell 1000 companies with lower price-to-book ratios and lower forecasted growth rates. An investor cannot invest directly in an index. The Russell 1000® Value Index measures the performance of the large cap value segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 1000 companies with relatively lower price-to-book ratios, lower I/B/E/S forecast medium term (2 year) growth and lower sales per share historical growth (5 years). The Russell 1000® Value Index is constructed to provide a comprehensive and unbiased barometer for the large cap value segment. The index is completely reconstituted annually to ensure new and growing equities are included and that the represented companies continue to reflect value characteristics.

data.title: Union Pacific: Norfolk Southern Deal Remains An Upside Catalyst
data.description:
Shares of Union Pacific Corporation (UNP) have delivered a total return of just 4% since the company's landmark proposed acquisition of Norfolk Southern (NSC) was announced on July 29, 2025. Comparably, the S&P 500 has delivered a total return of 10% over the same time period.

I view the proposed transaction with Norfolk Southern as a key positive catalyst for the stock over the next few years, which is not reflected in the share price currently. While the proposed merger faces headwinds, including the recent ruling by the U.S. Surface Transportation Board ("STB") and opposition from unions and competitors, my view is that it will ultimately be approved. The stock currently trades at a reasonable valuation, and I see scope for significant multiple expansion if the deal is approved. On the other hand, I see limited scope for valuation multiple compression in the event the deal is not approved by regulations. For these reasons, I rate the stock a Buy.

Norfolk Southern Transaction is a Game Changer

If approved, I believe that Union Pacific's acquisition of Norfolk Southern would be a game changer for the company. The deal would create America's first transcontinental railroad, which would immediately improve the combined company's competitive position relative to other railroads and trucking competitors. The companies believe that the proposed transcontinental railroad could provide 1-2 days of time savings on cross-country routes and better asset utilization, resulting in a better customer experience. In addition to providing benefits to customers, the deal is also expected to result in up to $2 billion of annual revenue synergies and $1 billion in cost synergies.

Currently, Union Pacific has a market cap of $136 billion, while Norfolk Southern has a market cap of $65 billion, and thus the ~$3 billion worth of annualized synergies is significant. The companies expect the transaction to be accretive to Adjusted EPS early in Year 2 following completion of the deal and accretive by a high single-digit percentage starting in Year 3 following the completion of the deal.

Consensus estimates for Union Pacific currently call for the company to deliver annual EPS growth of 9% and 9.9% for FY 2027 and 2028, respectively. Comparably, consensus estimates call for peers to deliver similar EPS growth over this period, and thus I do not believe the potential EPS accretion from the deal is reflected in Union Pacific EPS estimates. For example, CSX is expected to grow FY 2027 and 2028 EPS at annual rates of 12.6% and 11%, respectively. Canadian National Railway (CNI) is expected to grow FY 2027 and 2028 EPS at annual rates of 11.1% and 11%, respectively. Given these figures, to the extent the merger is approved in early 2027, I see upside to 2028 EPS estimates for Union Pacific and expect a greater acceleration in 2028 and 2029.

Merger Approval Headwinds

One reason why Union Pacific shares have thus far experienced a muted impact from the transaction announcement is the fact that the deal faces significant headwinds related to regulatory approval. One headwind has been the fact that a number of unions have recently come out against the deal. Specifically, in late December 2025, the Brotherhood of Locomotive Engineers and Trainmen and the Brotherhood of Maintenance of Way Employes Division both came out against the deal, citing safety and job concerns and the potential for higher shipping rates. Taken together, these two unions represent more than half of workers for Union Pacific and Norfolk Southern, and thus their opposition represents a key headwind. That said, it should be noted that SMART Transportation Division, which is the largest railroad union, has reached an agreement with Union Pacific to support the merger. Notably, Union Pacific agreed to make promises that employees would not face involuntary furloughs as a result of the merger. Moreover, Union Pacific was able to get SMART Transportation Division onboard with the merger after the union initially signaled opposition to the deal. While having all unions onboard with the proposed transaction is not a must, it would increase approval odds. Given this, I would not be surprised to see Union Pacific continue to negotiate over the next few months with Brotherhood of Locomotive Engineers and Trainmen and the Brotherhood of Maintenance of Way Employes Division in order to get them onboard with the transaction as well.

Another merger headwind is the opposition from competitors based on antitrust grounds. CSX, BNSF, and Canadian Pacific Kansas City have come out strongly against the proposed deal and filed requests with the Surface Transportation Board to reject the proposed Union Pacific Norfolk Southern deal and return it for more information. The fact that these competitors oppose the deal is not a surprise, as it would generally leave them in a weakened position relative to a combined Union Pacific-Norfolk Southern entity. Thus far, the arguments made against the transaction have carried at least some weight.

On January 16, 2026, the Surface Transportation Board sent back the proposed merger application, ruling it incomplete, saying that it lacked the required information required for the U.S. regulator to determine the competitive impact of the deal. Importantly, the board rejected the application without prejudice, allowing the application to be refiled once the deficiencies have been addressed. I expect Union Pacific and Norfolk Southern will be able to add the necessary information in order to satisfy the Surface Transportation Board information requests in the coming months.

One very important thing to note is that Union Pacific and Norfolk Southern have going for them is the fact that President Trump has already publicly backed the proposed merger. Trump fired one Democratic Surface Transportation Board member in mid-2025, and the board continues to have two vacancies, which Trump will be able to appoint, subject to Senate confirmation. As a result of control of the Surface Transportation Board, I expect President Trump will be able to exercise significant influence over the decision relating to the proposed Union Pacific acquisition of Norfolk Southern. For this reason, despite significant headwinds, I ultimately expect that the deal will receive regulatory approval despite objections from competitors and select unions.

Valuation is Attractive

Union Pacific shares currently trade at 18.3x consensus FY 2026 EPS. Comparably, the S&P 500 trades at 22.5x consensus forward earnings. Consensus estimates call for Union Pacific to deliver annual EPS growth of 7.2%, 9.4%, and 9.9% for FY 2026 - 2028. While this level of growth is somewhat below the near-term S&P 500 earnings growth estimates, I view Union Pacific as having similar long-term earnings prospects to the broader market. That said, given the capital-intensive nature of Union Pacific's business and high degree of cyclicality, I view a small valuation multiple discount to the S&P 500 as reasonable.

As shown by the chart below, Union Pacific shares are currently trading at a valuation discount to their average valuation over the past few years. I believe that this suggests that the market is somewhat skeptical that the proposed Norfolk Southern acquisition will ultimately be approved. Moreover, Union Pacific shares are also trading roughly in line with peers such as CSX, Canadian National Railway, and Canadian Pacific Kansas City. To the extent the Norfolk Southern deal does get approved, I expect that Union Pacific should have similar long-term growth prospects to these peers and thus should trade at a valuation multiple roughly in line with these peers. Historically, this has mostly been the case, though Canadian Pacific Kansas City has been able to drive better earnings growth over the past few years, which has helped drive a higher P/E multiple.

To the extent the proposed acquisition of Norfolk Southern is completed, I believe that synergies related to the deal should allow Union Pacific to deliver EPS growth, which is somewhat higher than peers in the years following the transaction's approval. A higher EPS growth outlook relative to peers argues for a higher valuation multiple, and thus I see scope for multiple expansion in the event the transaction's approval becomes more likely. Additionally, to the extent the transaction is approved, Union Pacific's long-term competitive advantage and moat relative to competitors would be stronger due to larger scale and a more differentiated network. As a result, I expect Union Pacific could trade at a valuation premium in the future to smaller peers. For these reasons, I see significant potential for valuation multiple expansion in the event the Norfolk Southern deal is approved. On the other hand, to the extent the deal is not approved, I do not expect Union Pacific shares to experience much valuation multiple contraction as the stock's current valuation is not stretched relative to the broader market, peers, or the stock's own historical valuation range.

Risks to Consider

In my view, the biggest near-term risk to Union Pacific shares would be a prolonged economic downturn. A long-lasting recession would be a significant negative for Union Pacific as volumes would decline and the pricing environment could become more competitive. Furthermore, Union Pacific has a significant amount of fixed costs and thus has significant margin contraction risk in the event that revenue declines. That said, any near-term earnings drop should be temporary, as Union Pacific has durable competitive advantages due to its unique network and relatively limited competition. I do not expect an economic slowdown in the near term and believe that recent Fed rate cuts have the potential to spur a better-than-expected macro environment in 2026.

Conclusion

Union Pacific shares have delivered fairly disappointing performance following the company's proposed transformational acquisition of Norfolk Southern. The deal faces significant headwinds from a regulatory perspective but has the support of President Trump.

Union Pacific shares are trading at a reasonable valuation relative to the broader market, peers, and their own historical valuation range. I see scope for significant multiple expansion in the event that the proposed Norfolk Southern deal gets completed. On the other hand, I see relatively limited downside risk to the stock's valuation multiple if the deal is not approved. For this reason, I view the stock's upside potential as attractive relative to downside potential. I rate the stock a Buy and believe the Norfolk Southern deal represents a key positive catalyst over the next few years.

data.title: RSPT: A Better Way To Invest In Technology Right Now
data.description:
2026 is off to a challenging start for Technology stocks, with the S&P 500 Technology names up slightly less than the S&P 500. The State Street Technology Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLK) is up 1.2%, while the S&P 500 has gained 1.4%. In 2025, XLK returned 24.6%, outpacing the 17.7% return of SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) and the 17.8% return of Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO). I expressed some concern about Technology stocks in November, suggesting that readers cut exposure to XLK and to Vanguard Information Technology Index Fund ETF Shares (VGT). I suggested that they consider cash and TIPS as an alternative, but I also recommended ProShares Russell 2000 Dividend Growers ETF (SMDV), which I do still like. While I am not a fan of Technology stocks right now, today I am discussing why Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Technology ETF (RSPT) should be a good alternative to these market cap-weighted Technology ETFs.

What RSPT Is

I have written previously about another Invesco ETF, the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (RSP). I mentioned in that article that the unique ETF, which I rated Hold, was a good alternative to the overall market due to its better diversification. I created an ETF model portfolio at year-end that held a 20% position. It was the second-largest position of the six that were included in it, though I have reduced it by 25% and now hold seven ETFs. RSP is currently 15.1%, and the model portfolio, which is measured against a balanced index that was 60% stocks and 40% bonds, currently has equity exposure of 40.7%.

In the RSP article, I discussed how the ETF, due to equal weighting rather than market-cap weighting, had just 14.9% Technology exposure compared to the S&P 500 having 34.6%. Well, RSPT, which is fully Technology names, is one of 11 sector ETFs for the S&P 500 that Invesco runs. Invesco launched it in late 2006. The company's RSPT fact sheet is updated as of 12/31, and it shows that the ETF has tracked the index well since inception, outpacing SPY but underperforming XLK.

The RSPT website page shares more information, including the weighting of its 71 Technology stocks as of 01/15. Here are the Top 10:

These top names made up 17.9% of the ETF, while they made up just 3.2% of XLK with a single position, Micron Technology (MU). XLK's top 10 represented 79.0% of the ETF, with the top 3 at 38.4% on 1/15.

Invesco's website shares the breakdown of RSPT by sector as of year-end:

XLK currently has 41% exposure to Semiconductors, and it has lower exposures than RSPT for Electronic Equipment, Instruments & Components.

RSPT currently has a net asset value of $4.1 billion, but it does not have that many followers at Seeking Alpha at 3660. XLK has over 76K. Invesco's management fee is 0.4%, which may seem high to some and is a lot higher than the XLK fee of 0.08% and the VGT fee of 0.09%.

How RSPT Has Performed

While RSPT is off to a good start in 2026 relative to XLK and VGT, rising 3.7% compared to XLK increasing 1.2% and VGT gaining 0.7%, it has lagged badly for the past few years. Looking at the action since the end of 2022, the X% total return is above the S&P 500, but it is way below its Technology peers:

The market cap-weighted ETFs have been helped by the Magnificent 7! Looking at the chart over the past three years, RSPT does not appear to be a bargain despite lagging XLK and VGT:

The ETF, at 47.18, is 65.3% above its 52-week low set in April and has three open gaps below the price, which is just under the all-time high set on Friday.

Why Swap Into RSPT

RSPT is for investors who want to be invested in large-cap Technology stocks but want to reduce the risk of the poor diversification of large-cap Technology ETFs like VGT or XLK. It holds the exact same names as XLK, but the equal weighting at rebalancing leaves it much better diversified. Investors in XLK have 38% exposure to three names: NVIDIA (NVDA), Apple (AAPL), and Microsoft (MSFT). All of these have outpaced RSPT over the past five years, though they are all down so far this year.

The really big impact from these stocks has been NVDA, which is about 14.8% of XLK, 17% of VGT, and just 1.46% of RSPT. Is NVDA a good stock to own? I don't think so, but this is not an article on NVDA. Holders of these large Technology ETFs that are market-cap weighted own a lot and should be comfortable owning such a large position. If NVDA keeps rallying, RSPT does own it!

Conclusion

I have initiated RSPT with a Hold, but I am suggesting that holders of large Technology stocks or the ETFs XLK and VGT consider swapping into it. I like what Invesco is doing with equal-weighted ETFs, though I am not a Technology fan right now. RSPT has less downside risk than the market cap-weighted ETFs.

I continue to prefer my Buy-rated SMDV, which is also equal-weighted but more diversified. If you are like me and bearish on stocks right now, cash seems like a good place! I am adding RSPT to my ETF Focus List, which now totals 80 ETFs.

data.title: ValuEngine Weekly Market Summary And Commentary
data.description:
Weekly Market Recap – Week Ending January 16, 2026

U.S. equity markets have begun 2026 with relatively muted index-level performance, but beneath the surface, leadership dynamics continue to evolve meaningfully.

The early days of the year are reinforcing the rotation trends that emerged late in 2025, as investors increasingly favor smaller-capitalization stocks, value-oriented strategies, and select cyclical sectors over the mega-cap growth stocks that dominated returns in recent years.

This environment has produced a widening dispersion of outcomes across asset classes, sectors, and styles, underscoring the importance of valuation discipline and diversification as market leadership broadens.

In the below tables, we use major ETFs as a proxy for some major indexes as well as each of the sector groups into which we divide the overall markets. Tracking these over time provides a more defined picture of the US markets than simply tracking major indexes. This is followed by notable individual stock movers over the past month, before presenting our full strategy outlook.

Strategy Note

Eleven days into 2026, U.S. equity markets have made little progress at the headline index level, continuing to trade largely sideways. Beneath the surface, however, the rotation trends that emerged during the fourth quarter of last year remain firmly in place. Leadership continues to shift toward small-cap and value-oriented stocks.

Small-cap equities have gotten off to a particularly strong start. The Russell 2000 (IWM) has gained 7.9% year to date, significantly outperforming large-cap benchmarks.

By comparison, the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO) has advanced just 1.2%, reflecting ongoing investor rotation away from the handful of mega-cap, primarily technology-driven stocks that dominated market performance over the past three years.

Positions in the former “Magnificent Seven” are increasingly being used as sources of funds for stocks with smaller capitalizations and more reasonable valuations.

The value-versus-growth divergence further reinforces this trend. The Vanguard Value ETF (VTV) has posted a 2.9% year-to-date gain, while the Vanguard Growth ETF (VUG) has declined 0.3% over the same period.

Foreign equities are also continuing to outpace U.S. large-cap stocks. The iShares MSCI EAFE ETF (EFA) is up 3.5% year to date, while the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ETF (EEM) has gained 5.8%. This relative strength supports the continuation of the weakening U.S. dollar theme that developed in 2025.

Commodities remain another area of strength. Precious metals, in particular, continue to attract investor interest. The SPDR Gold MiniShares Trust (GLDM) is up 6.3% year to date, while silver has delivered extraordinary gains. The iShares Silver Trust (SLV) has surged 25.8% in just the first eleven days of the year, pushing its trailing twelve-month return to an exceptional 189%.

One asset class that has reversed course relative to last year is digital assets. The iShares Bitcoin Trust (IBIT), which declined approximately 15% in 2025, has rebounded strongly and is up about 9% so far in 2026.

Given the crosscurrents across asset classes and styles, maintaining diversification remains a prudent approach. While rotation toward value and small-cap stocks is evident, leadership remains fluid.

Examining this theme more closely, the largest ETF capturing both value and small-cap exposure is the iShares Russell 2000 Value ETF (IWN). Although our model assigns IWN a below-average ValuEngine rating of 2 (Sell), three of its top ten holdings carry ValuEngine ratings of 5 (Strong Buy): Hecla Mining (HL), Commercial Metals Company (CMC), and Jackson Financial (JXN).

Despite its inclusion among these top-rated holdings, ValuEngine’s valuation model suggests that Hecla Mining appears overvalued at current price levels. In contrast, both Commercial Metals and Jackson Financial exhibit more reasonable valuations according to our models and may represent timely opportunities warranting further investigation.

data.title: Heartland Value Fund Q4 2025 Attribution Analysis & Portfolio Activity
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Heartland Value Fund Q4 2025 Portfolio Manager Commentary.

In the fourth quarter, the Heartland Value Fund gained 2.61%, compared with the 3.26% return for the Russell 2000® Value Index (RUJ). While the Fund slightly underperformed its benchmark in the quarter, it has outpaced the index over the past one, three, five, and 10 years and since inception in 1984.

During the quarter, stock selection accounted for most of our underperformance relative to the benchmark. Health Care accounted for nearly all the negative selection effect. Our results were more the outcome of what we didn’t own than our actual holdings in the sector. Biotech companies in the benchmark soared in October and November, which is a good example of the speculation which persists. However, we don’t have direct exposure to this group, as many are profitless. We are, though, actively examining our Health Care stocks as several were among our portfolio’s biggest detractors in the quarter.

An example of a Health Care holding that struggled in the quarter was Integer Holdings Corporation (ITGR), which specializes in the design, development, and production of medical technologies and components. In late October, Integer’s stock sank more than 30% after the company significantly lowered its outlook for growth and margin expansion in 2026, as several products experienced slower market adoption than was expected. But management expects these headwinds to be short-lived, with a robust portfolio of higher growth and margin products coming to market, which should allow the company to get back on track. The company still expects to outgrow its industry peers by 2% while growing earnings before interest and taxes (EBIT) at twice the rate of sales. Even better, the company announced a $200 million stock repurchase authorization after the selloff, increasing our conviction that the shares are materially undervalued. This confidence drove us to increase our exposure to Integer in the quarter.

Another position we increased was Columbus McKinnon (CMCO), which designs, manufactures, and distributes materials, handling products, and systems used in a variety of industrial applications, including lifting, automation, and precision conveyance. Early this year, CMCO reported slower-than-expected Q3 results, citing slowing industry demand driven by U.S. policy uncertainty. By itself, that would have likely triggered a mid-single-digit to high-single-digit selloff based on historic price reactions. However, management also announced a $2.7 billion acquisition of a competitor, Kito Crosby, which sent the stock plummeting more than 40% due to concerns surrounding execution and an increased leverage profile upon closing.

At current prices, we believe Columbus has a compelling risk/reward profile as management seeks to reduce leverage and improve the growth and margin profile of the consolidated business. Management is targeting mid-20% EBITDA margins upon integration, up from 15.5% in FY25 pre-acquisition. In our opinion, CMCO can rerate closer to its industrial peers that trade at an average of 12x EV/EBITDA. Meanwhile, our view of the stock’s attractive valuations seems to be validated by aggressive insider buying by management in the aftermath of the Q3 selloff.

A good illustration that true value can be realized with patience is Alexander & Baldwin (ALEX). When we originally initiated a position in this Hawaii-based real estate investment trust in the summer of 2024, we were significantly underweight in our Real Estate exposure, relative to our benchmark. In addition, the sector was the third-largest contributor to our portfolio’s “active risk” at the time. So, we sought to expand our exposure to the sector but were looking to do so in a cautious way.

From a risk/reward standpoint, ALEX looked favorable to most other REITs at the time. We liked how ALEX divested a non-core construction business, making this a pure-play REIT with assets strictly in Hawaii. And we were drawn to historically low industrial vacancies in the State as well as the company’s strong absorption rate relative to its peers.

Over the past six months, however, Alexander & Baldwin shares sold off materially over concerns of slowing tourism in Hawaii. This is despite the fact that little of ALEX’s properties are on resorts or directly exposed to travel. Actual real estate fundamentals in the Aloha State remain strong with industrial vacancies hovering at just around 2%. The company also enjoyed a robust pipeline of pre-leased developments to drive earnings growth and a strong balance sheet with below-average leverage, at 3.5x net debt to EBITDA.

Nevertheless, the stock was down more than 14% through the first two months of the quarter — until Dec. 8, when a group of investors agreed to take the company private at a 40% premium, which was close to our evaluation of fair value. While ALEX wasn’t a huge winner, the takeout boosted the holding’s performance to close out the year and demonstrated that other investors are starting to pay attention to the companies we focus on.

data.title: Nexus Industrial REIT: The Future Is Cautiously Bright
data.description:
Nexus Industrial REIT (NXR.UN:CA) is a Canadian pure-play industrial REIT. It recently disposed of its commercial and retail assets to focus solely on improving its existing industrial properties. As a result of this change in strategy, I believe management has taken some of the risk off the table, and with time, its stock price will gain momentum to move higher.

Nexus Industrial REIT: Past Year Updates

In the past year, there have been recent developments positioning Nexus Industrial REIT to be on the right track:

  • In August 2025, it sells 16 retail/office properties, resulting in $51M of proceeds
  • Some of the proceeds from the disposition are being used to develop its 325,000 SF expansion at St. Thomas, Ontario, and a 115,000 SF industrial building in Calgary, Alberta
  • In December 2025, Nexus Industrial REIT resumes its industrial acquisition program by acquiring 2 industrial properties in Quebec for $40.1 million. The anticipated stabilized market rate will be 10.4%. The transaction was funded from its credit facility.
  • In the past year, a 115,000 SF small bay industrial building at 102 Avenue Southwest, Calgary, was built on speculation. Its speculative bet paid off, and now 8/9 units are leased up, of which 5 are now firm. Once stabilized, the property is anticipated to produce an 11% unlevered return.
  • 150,000 SF industrial warehouse in Hamilton. The building currently sits vacant and is expected to generate a 5.9% yield. Nexus owns 80% of the building.
  • In Aug 2025, through its financial statement disclosures, Nexus Industrial REIT had increased credit facilities by a total of $360M:
    • Unsecured credit facilities increased by $160M from $625M to $785M
    • Term loan facility from $175M to $200M
    • Total revolving facility from $440M to $575M

The F-Score Continues to Be Just Okay

In reviewing its financial statements, the company's F-Score is listed below. The F-Score measures the financial strength of the company's balance sheet and income statements:

A score of 9 indicates a financially strong company, while a score closer to 0 suggests the company is financially weak. Nexus Industrial REIT hovers in the middle with a score of around 5–6.

One of the reasons for its low score is the accounting treatment of its Class B LP units and the fair market value of its industrial buildings. On an annual or periodic basis, it revalues these units/buildings, and if there is a fair market value loss, it has to reflect this in the income statements. This lowers the net income and creates a "fake" or notional loss. Although the company is operating as usual and collecting rents from its tenants, the fair market value adjustments are distorting its profitability. This is evident when net income in Q1-2025 had declined drastically to Q2-2025 (from $33,151 to -7,625 the following quarter), while its AFFO figures from quarter to quarter have been consistent, ranging from $14,000 to $16,000 each quarter.

Another hit to its F-Score is also Nexus Industrial REIT's quarterly issuance of new shares. Whenever the outstanding share count is more than the prior quarter/year, the F-Score takes a point off. However, issuing new shares is how management acquires new properties. It issues new shares to the property owners it is acquiring the property from. So this also distorts the F-Score downwards.

Another risk to also consider is it's not fully clear yet what the negative impact is on the loss of NOI from selling its portfolio of commercial/retail properties. There is a chance the payout ratio will have increased slightly in its Q4-2025 financials.

The Future is Cautiously Bright

Despite these risks, several factors may be working in the REIT's favor:

  • Nexus Industrial REIT is resuming its industrial acquisition program and purchasing assets that are accretive to its bottom line
  • The REIT is also internally upgrading its properties to increase NOI and locking into longer lease terms with existing tenants
  • It has increased its borrowing capacity by 25%, hinting at further acquisitions and developments in the near future
  • The current Canadian industrial market is taking a "wait and see" approach
    • This could mean some investors are sitting on the sidelines, giving more opportunities for Nexus Industrial REIT to acquire properties.
    • Overall vacancy rates across Canada remain flat, implying industrial tenants are showing resiliency to US tariffs and current economic conditions.
  • Nexus Industrial REIT had reduced its risk exposure by selling off its commercial/retail assets. Previously, management may have stretched itself too thin by being overly focused on more than one asset class. By refocusing its efforts on industrial assets alone, it no longer needs to deal with retail/commercial tenant turnovers, which are typically higher than in industrial tenants.

AFFO is a Concern, but a Mild One

Since 2023, Nexus Industrial REIT's AFFO payout has been more than 100%. This means cash has been going out faster than it is coming in:

A fellow Seeking Alpha writer had also highlighted this risk a few months ago. What is driving the higher than 100% AFFO payouts, I believe, are its Class B LP units. Whenever Nexus Industrial REIT acquires new properties, it issues Class B LP units to the property seller. It is set up this way to keep the debt on Nexus Industrial REIT's balance sheet low, and because these units are kept one level above the Nexus Industrial REIT's financial books, it also does not impact NOI.

To be clear, it is not structured this way to manipulate the books but to incentivize property owners to sell to Nexus Industrial REIT and to allow sellers to participate in the monthly REIT distributions.

In looking at Q3-2025, its payout ratio was 107%; this slippage amounts to a little more than $2M that quarter. For now, Nexus Industrial REIT can manage this payout, and I believe over time, as it engages in more accretive acquisitions and through tenant step rents, the payout ratio should steadily decline.

Investors Should Be Patient Here

It was only August 2025 when Nexus Industrial REIT had shed its portfolio of commercial/retail properties. Investors should give management time as it realigns its strategy to be a pure-play industrial player.

Its long-term debt has been below 50% for the past few years, which shows the balance sheet has bandwidth to make further acquisitions. Since the REIT is a growth-oriented operator, management will most likely never increase its dividends even if its payout ratio returns to sub-100%.

However, industrial assets are typically a much safer asset class than retail and commercial. Also, management seems to have a knack for finding properties that are accretive and boost its bottom line. If investors can buy into this investment thesis, then the current stock price could be considered undervalued. If investors are willing to show patience and give management time to roll out its industrial pure-play strategy, then in the long run, the stock will start increasing again.

I'm bullish on Nexus Industrial REIT.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ClearBridge Growth Strategy Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
By Evan Bauman, Aram Green & Amanda Leithe, CFA

Balance Matters When Markets Rotate - Market Overview

U.S. equities delivered resilient but volatile performance in the fourth quarter. While major indexes remained close to all-time highs, market conditions shifted meaningfully beneath the surface. Momentum-driven themes tied to artificial intelligence (AI), technology and cryptocurrencies extended into the early part of the quarter before reversing, resulting in heightened volatility and greater stock-level dispersion.

The S&P 500 Index returned 2.7% during the quarter, while the benchmark Russell Midcap Growth Index declined 3.7%. As volatility increased, investors became more selective, rewarding companies with durable fundamentals, innovation-driven growth and strong execution, while penalizing stocks where expectations had moved ahead of near-term fundamentals.

Against this backdrop, the ClearBridge Growth Strategy outperformed its benchmark in the fourth quarter, marking its third consecutive quarter of outperformance. Results reflected participation in early-quarter momentum — particularly from AI-related exposures — combined with strong downside protection as risk appetite moderated later in the period. We believe this outcome underscores the benefits of a balanced approach to growth investing.

2025 Year in Review

Looking back, 2025 was characterized by a wide range of market conditions that tested portfolio construction and reinforced the importance of discipline and flexibility. The year began with elevated uncertainty and the broad market endured a sharp correction from mid-February through early April, followed by a powerful rebound driven by enthusiasm around AI and innovation-led growth. After hitting an interim high in late October, markets became more volatile with rapid rotations and reversals putting a premium on portfolio diversity and our ability as managers to remain nimble, tactical and convicted in our decision-making.

Overall, the Strategy delivered a strong year, outperforming its benchmark by nearly 600 basis points (gross of fees). Market outcomes among the mid and large cap stocks we target increasingly reflected idiosyncratic drivers rather than broad factor exposure, rewarding companies investing in innovation, improving productivity and executing well against long-term growth opportunities.

In a market where much of the upside is priced in, stock selection matters more than ever.

Importantly, 2025 also demonstrated the benefits of a multiyear evolution of the portfolio. While our core philosophy remains unchanged after more than four decades, the Strategy today reflects a more balanced expression of growth. The steady compounders that continue to make up the base of our portfolio can provide effective downside capture for investors, while some of the disruptive growth names that have been added over the past few years have helped us better keep pace in a rising market. The portfolio is also more diversified across sectors and subsectors to better balance conviction with risk management. We believe this positioning has improved the portfolio’s resilience while preserving its ability to capture long-term upside for clients.

Quarterly Performance

Fourth-quarter outperformance was driven by a combination of balanced portfolio construction and strong stock selection. Early in the quarter, momentum tied to AI and technology supported results, while the portfolio’s exposure to steadier compounders and defensive growth names helped protect performance as volatility increased.

Within IT, Shopify (SHOP), an e-commerce platform that enables merchants to operate and scale digital storefronts, benefited from continued adoption by larger enterprises and improving monetization across its ecosystem. Broadcom, a leading semiconductor company and long-term holding, continued to execute well amid strong demand for custom silicon supporting AI workloads. TE Connectivity (TEL), an electronic component manufacturer specializing in connectivity and sensor solutions, also contributed, supported by secular trends in its automotive and industrial businesses.

While communication services was the greatest detractor within the benchmark, our differentiated positioning supported relative outperformance. For example, a number of portfolio holdings in sports and live media, such as Madison Square Garden Sports (MSGS), Madison Square Garden Entertainment (MSGE) and TKO Group (TKO), were positive contributors in the quarter.

However, select holdings in consumer staples and health care did face pressure. Following a period of strong performance, shares of e.l.f. Beauty (ELF), a cosmetics company focused on affordable, digitally native products, declined as sales growth was hurt by management’s decision to stop shipments to retailers that were slow to pass through tariff-related price increases. That said, we are encouraged that consumption trends for the brand remain healthy and management is already seeing shipment growth recover in the current quarter. In health care, Doximity, a digital platform serving medical professionals, traded lower as higher-beta growth stocks within health care experienced renewed volatility.

Positive contributions from steadier growth holdings helped to offset these pressures. In the consumer discretionary sector, TJX Companies (TJX), an off-price retailer, delivered solid third-quarter results and had a strong start to the fourth quarter as better merchandise availability yielded broader and higher-quality assortments that supported traffic, same-store sales and earnings growth. Our consumer performance also benefited from the recent addition of On Holding (ONON), a premium athletic footwear and apparel company gaining share globally. On Holding delivered an impressive quarter punctuated by strong growth in international markets, such as China, as well as accelerating growth from apparel. Likewise, within health care, top individual contributor Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX) benefited from growing optimism around its kidney disease pipeline and continued to demonstrate the value of idiosyncratic, innovation-driven growth.

Portfolio Positioning

Overall, 2025 was a period of strong idea generation that saw the Strategy reallocate capital toward opportunities with more attractive risk-reward profiles. These included new positions in merchant power producer Vistra (VST), biotech company Alnylam Pharmaceuticals (ALNY) and global hospitality company Hilton (HLT), among others. Staying true to our sell discipline, we also exited a number of holdings where we had diminished conviction in the company’s growth outlook such as health care provider UnitedHealth Group and audiovisual technology company Dolby.

Additionally, we looked for opportunities to elevate the quality of the portfolio. Within industrials, for instance, we rotated our exposure in the fourth quarter from Old Dominion Freight Line, a leading less-than-truckload (LTL) carrier, into XPO (XPO), the fourth-largest LTL provider in North America. We remain mindful of the still-challenging macro environment for freight and see greater idiosyncratic growth potential at XPO. Under new leadership, the company is improving service levels, pricing discipline and margins, leaving more room for outperformance should macro malaise persist.

We also continued to use our profitable larger positions in Broadcom (AVGO) and Robinhood Markets (HOOD) to opportunistically fund more attractively valued holdings. Most recently this included adds to Freeport-McMoRan (FCX), a global copper producer and one of our top contributors for the quarter, following a price drawdown related to an operational incident late in the third quarter. We believe management handled the situation effectively, and the dislocation reinforced our view that duration can be an advantage when owning high-quality assets tied to long-term secular demand.

Outlook

Our outlook remains consistent with what we articulated earlier in the year — and importantly, what played out in the fourth quarter. Markets remain resilient, but volatility has increased as momentum-driven areas cooled and stock-level dispersion widened, reinforcing the importance of disciplined stock selection and a balanced approach to portfolio construction.

AI continues to represent a powerful long-term opportunity, though early beneficiaries such as semiconductors and infrastructure have already seen significant gains. We are focused on making sure we have not only the right exposure within the AI complex but also other offensive bets in the portfolio to position the Strategy well should market leadership broaden further. At the same time, we continue to emphasize balance, owning companies not only with offensive growth potential but also defensive characteristics built on strong free cash flow, clean balance sheets and proven management teams, which should help to protect the portfolio should the environment weaken.

We remain opportunistic — taking advantage of pockets when stocks are oversold and trimming when enthusiasm runs ahead of fundamentals — while staying committed to a bottom-up, high active share, and long-term-oriented approach. We believe this discipline positions the Strategy well to navigate continued volatility, identify durable winners beyond broad market trends, and drive long-term performance for clients.

Portfolio Highlights

The ClearBridge Growth Strategy outperformed its Russell Midcap Growth Index during the fourth quarter. On a relative basis, the Strategy had positive contributions from two of the nine sectors in which it was invested. The contributors were the consumer discretionary and materials sectors, while the consumer staples and information technology (IT) sectors detracted the most.

On a relative basis, overall stock contributed to performance, while sector allocation effects detracted. Stock selection in the IT, communication services and consumer discretionary sectors, as well as an overweight to the materials sector, benefited performance. Conversely, stock selection in the consumer staples, financials and health care sectors, overweights to the communication services and IT sectors and an underweight to the consumer discretionary sector weighed on performance.

On an individual stock basis, the biggest contributors to relative returns were Vertex Pharmaceuticals, Broadcom, Freeport-McMoRan, TE Connectivity and an underweight to Roblox (RBLX). The largest detractors from relative returns were e.l.f. Beauty, Doximity, Robinhood Markets and a lack of exposure to Rocket Lab (RKLB) and Expedia (EXPE).

Evan Bauman, Managing Director, Portfolio Manager

Aram Green, Managing Director, Portfolio Manager

Amanda Leithe, CFA, Managing Director, Portfolio Manager
